spans.content: Context-Aware Multimodal Pipeline in RAG-
Anything
RAG-Anything augments each document unit with its local context before indexing, so that, for example, an
image or table is not processed in isolation but together with nearby text. In practice this is implemented
via a ContextExtractor component (configurable via RAGAnythingConfig ). During parsing, every
content item (text chunk, image, table, equation, etc.) is tagged and stored with its position. The
ContextExtractor then gathers surrounding text according to a user-specified window (e.g. neighboring
pages or chunks) and mode (e.g. “page”, “chunk” or token-based)
. This textual context (including
optional headings or captions) is prepended to the item before further processing. For example, an image’s
caption or the preceding paragraph can be included so that a vision model sees the image and its caption
together. This “context-aware” generation step (controlled by parameters like
context_window ,
context_mode , and
max_context_tokens ) ensures that the descriptive text produced for each
multimodal item accurately reflects its role in the larger document
Atomic Unit Processing & Embedding
After parsing, the content is split into atomic units by modality. Plain text is broken into chunks (typically by
tokens or layout) and handled by LightRAG’s text pipeline (semantic chunking, embedding, NER, relation
extraction, etc.). For non-text units (images, tables, equations), RAG-Anything uses dedicated modal
processors: e.g. an
ImageModalProcessor
uses a vision-language model (VLM) to produce a detailed
caption and an
“entity
summary”
for each figure; a
TableModalProcessor
or
EquationModalProcessor
uses LLMs to extract structured descriptions. Crucially, before calling the
model, each processor invokes the ContextExtractor to prepend nearby text, headers, or captions to the
input prompt. The output of this stage is twofold: (1) for each multimodal item
c _ { j }
a detailed description
d _ { c h u n k _ { j } }
optimized for retrieval, and (2) an entity summary capturing key attributes (names, types,eentityj
etc.) used for graph construction
All chunks and graph elements are then embedded: RAG-Anything builds a single dense vector table
containing embeddings of every text chunk, every graph node, and even graph relationships. In particular,
after extracting entities and relations (see below), the system embeds all graph vertices and edges into the
unified vector space using the chosen embedding model . This means that retrieval can match on entire 3
chunks, on individual entities, or on relations, since all are represented by vectors.
Knowledge Graph Construction (Entities & Links)
The next stage constructs a multimodal knowledge graph from the processed content. Two
complementary graphs are built and then fused:
Cross-Modal Graph (Non-Text Units): For each non-text item•
c _ { j }
(image, table, equation, etc.), RAG-
Anything creates a multimodal entity node
v _ { m m _ { j } }
. It then runs an information-extraction pipeline on

Context-Aware Multimodal Pipeline in RAG-
Anything
RAG-Anything augments each document unit with its local context before indexing, so that, for example, an
image or table is not processed in isolation but together with nearby text. In practice this is implemented

content item (text chunk, image, table, equation, etc.) is tagged and stored with its position. The
ContextExtractor then gathers surrounding text according to a user-specified window (e.g. neighboring
pages or chunks) and mode (e.g. “page”, “chunk” or token-based)
. This textual context (including
optional headings or captions) is prepended to the item before further processing. For example, an image’s
caption or the preceding paragraph can be included so that a vision model sees the image and its caption
together. This “context-aware” generation step (controlled by parameters like
context_window ,
context_mode , and
max_context_tokens ) ensures that the descriptive text produced for each
multimodal item accurately reflects its role in the larger document

ImageModalProcessor
uses a vision-language model (VLM) to produce a detailed
caption and an
“entity
summary”

EquationModalProcessor

input prompt. The output of this stage is twofold: (1) for each multimodal item
c _ { j }

d _ { c h u n k _ { j } }
optimized for retrieval, and (2) an entity summary capturing key attributes (names, types,eentityj
etc.) used for graph construction
All chunks and graph elements are then embedded: RAG-Anything builds a single dense vector table
containing embeddings of every text chunk, every graph node, and even graph relationships. In particular,
after extracting entities and relations (see below), the system embeds all graph vertices and edges into the
unified vector space using the chosen embedding model . This means that retrieval can match on entire 3
chunks, on individual entities, or on relations, since all are represented by vectors.
Knowledge Graph Construction (Entities & Links)
The next stage constructs a multimodal knowledge graph from the processed content. Two
complementary graphs are built and then fused:
Cross-Modal Graph (Non-Text Units): For each non-text item•
c _ { j }
(image, table, equation, etc.), RAG-
Anything creates a multimodal entity node
v _ { m m _ { j } }
. It then runs an information-extraction pipeline on
the item’s description
d _ { c h u n k _ { j } }
to identify intra-chunk entities (e.g. objects in an image, variables in an
equation) and their relations
. Those fine-grained entities4
V _ { j }
and relations
E _ { j }
become sub-nodes
and edges. Critically, each such entity is linked back to the parent item node with an explicit
belongs_to edge. In other words, if a chart contains the entities “GDP” and “year”, the graph will
have nodes for those terms and edges (GDP) -[belongs_to]-> (Chart1)
. This preserves 2
modality grounding: the image/table node serves as an anchor for its contained concepts. Thanks to
the earlier context injection, the extraction also captures textual cues from surrounding context (e.g.
figure captions or nearby mentions).

equation) and their relations
. Those fine-grained entities4
V _ { j }

E _ { j }

and edges. Critically, each such entity is linked back to the parent item node with an explicit
belongs_to edge. In other words, if a chart contains the entities “GDP” and “year”, the graph will
have nodes for those terms and edges (GDP) -[belongs_to]-> (Chart1)
. This preserves 2
modality grounding: the image/table node serves as an anchor for its contained concepts. Thanks to
the earlier context injection, the extraction also captures textual cues from surrounding context (e.g.
figure captions or nearby mentions).
Text-Based Graph (Text Chunks): Independently, every text chunk is processed with standard NLP•
(NER
relation extraction) to build a text-centric knowledge graph. This graph encodes entities and
relationships found purely in the document’s text. Since text already carries rich semantics, this
pipeline does not need the extra cross-modal context step.
Graph Fusion: The two graphs are merged by entity alignment: nodes from the multimodal graph•
and the text graph with the same or similar names are matched (e.g. the entity “enzyme” in a chart
description vs “enzyme” in the body text). These alignments unify the graphs into a single knowledge
graph
G
spanning all modalities
. The fused graph thus encodes intra-chunk relationships
(from tables/figures) and document-wide textual relations, all linked through shared entity nodes.
Retrieval Over Entities, Relations, and Chunks
During query time, RAG-Anything uses a hybrid retrieval strategy leveraging the unified graph and the
embedding table. The query is first encoded (with awareness of any modality cues, e.g. if it mentions
“figure” or “table”) into the same embedding space. Then two complementary searches run in parallel:
Structural (Graph) Retrieval: The system scans the knowledge graph for exact matches of entities•
or keywords in the query. Matching nodes are selected, and their local graph neighborhood is
expanded (multi-hop) up to a configurable distance. Because of the belongs_to chains and
extracted relations, this structural walk retrieves connected entities, relations, and ultimately the
content chunks (text or multimodal) they belong to. For instance, a query about “fermentation”
might hit an entity node in a figure, then follow a belongs_to edge to retrieve the figure’s
description and any related text chunks
Semantic (Vector) Retrieval: Simultaneously, the query’s embedding is used to perform a nearest-•
neighbor search in the unified embedding table. This returns the top-k semantically similar items
across all modalities – whether they are whole text chunks, image captions, or even relation-
descriptions. Because graph entities and relations were also embedded, the search can match on
them too, surfacing relevant content that lacks explicit graph links.
The two result sets are then merged. RAG-Anything applies a fusion scoring that balances graph-derived
signals (e.g. how central an entity is in the graph) and embedding similarity, along with any query-specified
modality preferences
. This multi-signal fusion yields a final ranked list of chunks and entity contexts,
maximizing both structural relevance and semantic fit.
Text-Based Graph (Text Chunks): Independently, every text chunk is processed with standard NLP•
(NER
relation extraction) to build a text-centric knowledge graph. This graph encodes entities and
relationships found purely in the document’s text. Since text already carries rich semantics, this
pipeline does not need the extra cross-modal context step.
Graph Fusion: The two graphs are merged by entity alignment: nodes from the multimodal graph•
and the text graph with the same or similar names are matched (e.g. the entity “enzyme” in a chart
description vs “enzyme” in the body text). These alignments unify the graphs into a single knowledge
graph
G
spanning all modalities
. The fused graph thus encodes intra-chunk relationships
(from tables/figures) and document-wide textual relations, all linked through shared entity nodes.
Retrieval Over Entities, Relations, and Chunks
During query time, RAG-Anything uses a hybrid retrieval strategy leveraging the unified graph and the
embedding table. The query is first encoded (with awareness of any modality cues, e.g. if it mentions
“figure” or “table”) into the same embedding space. Then two complementary searches run in parallel:
Structural (Graph) Retrieval: The system scans the knowledge graph for exact matches of entities•
or keywords in the query. Matching nodes are selected, and their local graph neighborhood is
expanded (multi-hop) up to a configurable distance. Because of the belongs_to chains and
extracted relations, this structural walk retrieves connected entities, relations, and ultimately the
content chunks (text or multimodal) they belong to. For instance, a query about “fermentation”
might hit an entity node in a figure, then follow a belongs_to edge to retrieve the figure’s
description and any related text chunks
Semantic (Vector) Retrieval: Simultaneously, the query’s embedding is used to perform a nearest-•
neighbor search in the unified embedding table. This returns the top-k semantically similar items
across all modalities – whether they are whole text chunks, image captions, or even relation-
descriptions. Because graph entities and relations were also embedded, the search can match on
them too, surfacing relevant content that lacks explicit graph links.
The two result sets are then merged. RAG-Anything applies a fusion scoring that balances graph-derived
signals (e.g. how central an entity is in the graph) and embedding similarity, along with any query-specified
modality preferences
. This multi-signal fusion yields a final ranked list of chunks and entity contexts,
maximizing both structural relevance and semantic fit.
Benefits for Multimodal Documents
By grounding every unit in context and integrating everything into one graph-plus-embedding index, RAG-
Anything handles complex PDFs (with interleaved text, images, tables, etc.) far more accurately than text-
only RAG. For example, an image in a PDF often comes with a caption or is referenced by a nearby
paragraph; the ContextExtractor ensures the image’s caption and adjacent text are included when the
image is described. This disambiguates visual content (an unlabeled chart becomes meaningful when its
legend is included) and links it to the proper part of the document. The graph’s belongs_to edges mean
that a query hitting an entity inside an image will still retrieve the entire figure and its caption. Likewise, any
sidecar metadata (such as figure captions or surrounding headings) can be folded into context. In short,
RAG-Anything’s context-aware pipeline weaves together all modalities, so no relevant information is
dropped. Empirically, this yields better retrieval on long documents with mixed content, because the model
sees the full multimodal context of each piece
2 \quad 1
Configuration and Integration
In code, context-aware processing is enabled by settings in the
RAGAnythingConfig . By default,
context_window=1
and
context_mode="page"
(meaning each item looks at the rest of its page), but
these can be tuned per dataset. Users also enable or disable each modality (images, tables, equations) in
the config. The RAGAnything framework automatically wires the ContextExtractor into each multimodal
processor – no extra coding is needed beyond configuration. For example:
config
RAGAnythingConfig(
context_window
\ L = \ L \mathcal { 1 }
# how many pages/chunks around each item to use
context_mode
"page", # or "chunk" for neighbor blocks, or "token" for max
tokens
max_context_tokens
enable_image_processing
True,
enable_table_processing
\underline { { \underline { { \mathbf { \Pi } } } } } =
True,
enable_equation_processing
\underline { { \underline { { \mathbf { \Pi } } } } } =
True,
rag
RAGAnything(config)
rag.index_documents([...]) # builds the graph
index using context-aware
processing
In practice, one simply supplies multimodal documents (PDFs, images, etc.) and RAG-Anything’s end-to-end
pipeline (parsing
content analysis
graph/index building
hybrid retrieval) runs with context-
awareness built in. The result is a unified index where images, text, tables and their metadata are all
interlinked, yielding more accurate and disambiguated RAG retrieval. 2
